items.correct: No - there is no evidence for horned battle helmets; surviving examples are simple iron caps, and the horns were a nineteenth-century artistic invention.
items.misconception: Viking warriors charged into battle wearing horned helmets.
items.random_wrong: Vikings never wore helmets of any kind.
items.why: Costume drama and opera imagery is so strong that students treat it as evidence; archaeology tells a different story.

items.q: Were the Vikings only violent raiders?
items.correct: No - they were also traders, farmers, craftworkers and settlers who founded towns such as Dublin and developed Jorvik, trading as far as the Islamic world.
items.misconception: Vikings did nothing but raid monasteries and burn villages.
items.random_wrong: Vikings refused to trade with Christians on principle.
items.why: Monks wrote the surviving accounts of raids, so the sources exaggerate one side of a society that archaeology shows was deeply commercial.

items.q: Was Columbus the first European to reach North America?
items.correct: No - Norse voyagers reached Vinland around the year 1000, and the settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland provides archaeological proof.
items.misconception: No European set foot in the Americas before Columbus in 1492.
items.random_wrong: The Vikings colonised the whole of North America for centuries.
items.why: The Columbus story dominates popular memory; the excavated Norse site shows earlier contact, though it was small-scale and short-lived.

items.q: Which monastery was famously raided by Vikings in 793?
items.why: Correct answer: Lindisfarne. “Iona” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why were longships so effective for raiding?
items.correct: Their shallow draught let them travel up rivers and land on beaches for surprise attacks
items.misconception: They were armoured with iron plates
items.random_wrong: They carried cannon on their decks
items.why: It's Their shallow draught let them travel up rivers and land on beaches for surprise attacks. “They were armoured with iron plates” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The part of England where Danish law and customs operated after Viking settlement
items.misconception: The tax the English paid to make Vikings leave
items.random_wrong: A law code written by King Cnut for Denmark
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The part of England where Danish law and customs operated after Viking settlement. “The tax the English paid to make Vikings leave” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who led the Norse settlement of Greenland around 985?
items.why: Correct answer: Erik the Red. “Cnut” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Lindisfarne raided?
items.why: The real figure is 793 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
